cards.back: A system maps an input signal x(t) to an output signal y(t). It processes, transforms, or modifies signals.
Both additivity (response to sum = sum of responses) AND scaling (αx → αy). Together: superposition principle.
If x₁→y₁ and x₂→y₂, then x₁+x₂ → y₁+y₂. The response to a sum is the sum of individual responses.
If x(t)→y(t), then αx(t)→αy(t) for any scalar α. Also called homogeneity.
Shifting the input by τ shifts the output by the same τ. If x(t)→y(t), then x(t−τ)→y(t−τ). The system's behaviour doesn't change with time.
Linear Time-Invariant. A system that satisfies both superposition (linearity) and time invariance.

cards.front: Why is LTI the most important system class?
cards.back: LTI systems can be fully analysed using Fourier/Laplace transforms and convolution. They're characterised entirely by their impulse response h(t).
No. Scaling fails: (αx)² = α²x² ≠ αx². Squaring is a nonlinear operation.
Linear (superposition holds: t[αx₁+βx₂]=αtx₁+βtx₂). NOT time-invariant: x(t−τ) gives tx(t−τ) ≠ (t−τ)x(t−τ).
No. Zero input gives y=1≠0, violating scaling (αx→αy with α=0). It's affine, not linear. Constant offsets break linearity.

cards.front: Is differentiation y(t)=dx/dt an LTI operation?
cards.back: Yes. Linear: d/dt[αx₁+βx₂]=αdx₁/dt+βdx₂/dt. Time-invariant: d/dt[x(t−τ)]=x'(t−τ)=y(t−τ). Both properties satisfied.

cards.front: How do you test a system for LTI?
cards.back: Step 1: Test superposition (apply α₁x₁+α₂x₂, check if output = α₁y₁+α₂y₂). Step 2: Test TI (replace t with t−τ in input, check if output shifts by same τ). Both must pass.
